senses.definition: Relating to or being an amacrine cell in the nervous system, especially in the retina of the eye.
examples: The researcher measured amacrinal activity in the inner layers of the retina.
Certain visual disorders are linked to abnormal amacrinal function.

senses.definition: Relating to or being an amacrine cell, a type of nerve cell in the inner part of the retina.
examples: Amacrine cells help shape how visual signals are processed before they reach the brain.
The inner plexiform layer contains many amacrine and bipolar cells.

senses.definition: An amacrine cell in the retina that connects with other nerve cells and helps process visual information.
examples: Each amacrine plays a role in integrating signals from many bipolar cells.
The study classified several types of amacrines based on their shape and connections.

senses.definition: A fool or very silly person, especially in Irish or Scottish English.
examples: Don’t be such an amadan and listen to the advice you’re given.
They laughed and called him an amadan for walking out in the storm.

senses.definition: A small red and brown waxbill bird kept as a cage bird, also called the red avadavat.
examples: The market was once full of cages holding brightly colored amadavat.
An amadavat fluttered nervously as the dealer approached the cage.

senses.definition: Plural of amadavat, referring to more than one of these small waxbill birds.
examples: Traders once exported amadavats in large numbers from South Asia.
Several amadavats were feeding quietly among the tall grasses.

senses.definition: Having stamens that are not united into definite groups, used in botanical description.
examples: The species was described as amadelphous in the original botanical text.
Amadelphous flowers contrast with those whose stamens are clearly grouped.

senses.definition: A family name or given name of African origin, found in several countries.
examples: Dr. Amadi presented her findings at the conference.
Amadi grew up in a small coastal town before moving to the city.

senses.definition: The name of a heroic knight in medieval and Renaissance romance literature, especially in the tale Amadis of Gaul.
examples: The old romance told of Amadis and his many trials for love.
Writers often compared the brave knight to Amadis of Gaul.

senses.definition: A plural form of a word meaning men in some Southern African languages, sometimes used in English-language contexts.
examples: The elders called the amadoda to discuss the problem.
Songs were sung by the amadoda as they worked together.

senses.definition: A spongy, dry material from a fungus, used historically as tinder to start fires.
examples: The traveller kept a piece of amadou in his pouch for lighting fires.
Amadou catches a spark easily and burns slowly.

senses.definition: Plural of amadou, referring to several pieces or types of this tinder material.
examples: Different amadous were tested for how quickly they would light.
They stored amadous in a dry leather bag.

senses.definition: A figure in Welsh mythology, sometimes described as a god linked with agriculture or farming.
examples: Amaethon appears in a number of early Welsh tales.
The story tells how Amaethon stole magical animals from the Otherworld.

senses.definition: A regional or ethnic term used in Southern Africa, sometimes for a group of migrant workers or people from another area.
examples: Older residents still use the word amafingo for newcomers to the mines.
Songs from the amafingo reflected their mixed backgrounds.

senses.definition: The name of a town or locality in some countries, sometimes mentioned in place names or addresses.
examples: They moved from Amaga to the capital in search of work.
Coffee from the hills around Amaga is well known.

senses.definition: A woman employed as a live-in servant or child-care worker, especially in parts of Asia; now mainly historical or regional.
examples: She grew up with an amah who cared for her while her parents worked.
In old colonial houses, the amah often slept near the children’s room.

senses.definition: Plural of amah, referring to several women working as live-in servants or child-care workers.
examples: During that period many foreign families hired amahs to look after their homes.
The quarters for the amahs were small and crowded.

senses.definition: A member of an Indigenous people living in parts of the Amazon region of Peru and Brazil.
examples: The Amahuaca have maintained many of their traditional customs.
Researchers worked closely with Amahuaca elders when documenting the language.

senses.definition: The language spoken by the Amahuaca people.
examples: Only a few hundred people now speak Amahuaca fluently.
She is preparing a dictionary of Amahuaca.

senses.definition: With great speed or force; very quickly or strongly, now mainly literary or archaic.
examples: The wind blew amain and shook the old house.
They rushed amain towards the city gates.

senses.definition: An older or variant form of amain, meaning with great speed or force; now archaic.
examples: The rain poured amaine upon the fields.
Men rode amaine to carry the warning.

senses.definition: A rare variant of almost, used in some older or dialect writing.
examples: The work was amaist finished when the storm came.
He was amaist asleep by the time they arrived.

senses.definition: To overcome, defeat, or gain control over someone or something; now archaic or literary.
examples: No man could amaister his fierce temper.
They sought to amaister the rebels before nightfall.

senses.definition: A regional term in parts of Africa, used for particular groups or clans in some local languages.
examples: The amakebe gathered at the chief’s homestead.
Stories about the amakebe are passed down through song.

senses.definition: An older or less common spelling of Xhosa, referring to the Xhosa people of South Africa.
examples: Travelers wrote about the customs of the amakosa.
The amakosa resisted colonial rule in several wars.

senses.definition: Plural of inkosi in some Nguni languages, used in English contexts for chiefs or traditional leaders.
examples: The amakosi met to discuss land issues with the government.
Villagers turned to the amakosi for guidance.

senses.definition: A strongly negative South African slang term for foreign African migrants, considered offensive.
examples: He was insulted when someone called him an amakwerekwere.
Campaigners urged people to stop using the word amakwerekwere.

senses.definition: A common given name of Arabic origin used for both men and women, often meaning hope.
examples: Amal will join us for the meeting this afternoon.
The novel follows Amal as she navigates life in a new country.

senses.definition: A West African dish made from yam, cassava, or plantain flour, cooked into a smooth, stretchy dough and eaten with soup or stew.
examples: We had amala and okra soup for dinner.
She showed me how to stir the amala until it became smooth.

senses.definition: A regional or historical term whose specific reference depends on local usage, sometimes used as a group or clan name.
examples: The amalaita played a key role in the dispute.
Songs of the amalaita are still performed at festivals.

senses.definition: A round, ribbed stone disk on top of a column or temple tower in traditional Indian architecture.
examples: The amalaka crowns the spire of the temple.
Art historians studied the carvings on the amalaka.

senses.definition: A member of an ancient people mentioned in the Bible who lived in the region south of Canaan.
examples: The text describes a battle between the Israelites and the Amalekites.
Scholars debate the historical identity of the Amalekite nation.

senses.definition: Relating to the Amalekites or their land in biblical texts.
examples: The story takes place in Amalekite territory.
He studied Amalekite customs as described in scripture.

senses.definition: A rare or obsolete spelling of amulet, an object believed to protect its owner.
examples: He wore an amalett around his neck for protection.
In the old tale, a silver amalett saves the hero from harm.

senses.definition: Relating to the town of Amalfi in Italy or its people.
examples: They admired the Amalfian coast with its steep cliffs.
Amalfian traders once sailed widely across the Mediterranean.

senses.definition: A person from Amalfi in Italy.
examples: An Amalfian guided us through the narrow streets.
Many Amalfians made their living from the sea.

senses.definition: Relating to Amalfi in Italy, especially its medieval maritime republic and culture.
examples: The museum holds many Amalfitan documents and maps.
Amalfitan ships were once a common sight in Mediterranean ports.

senses.definition: A person from Amalfi in Italy.
examples: An Amalfitan welcomed us with a smile.
The Amalfitans developed a strong maritime tradition.

senses.definition: An informal shortened form of amalgam, meaning a mixture of different things.
examples: The city is an amalg of cultures and languages.
Her style is an odd amalg of formal and casual.

senses.definition: An alloy made by mixing mercury with another metal, sometimes used for dental fillings.
examples: The dentist removed the old amalgam filling.
Silver amalgam has been used in dentistry for many years.

senses.definition: A mixture or combination of different elements forming a single whole, often used formally.
examples: The festival is an amalgam of music, food, and art.
Her work is an unusual amalgam of fact and fiction.

senses.definition: Able to be combined or merged into a single whole.
examples: The two departments are clearly amalgamable.
These technologies are not easily amalgamable with existing systems.

senses.definition: To join or combine two or more things, especially organizations, into one.
examples: The two firms decided to amalgamate to reduce costs.
Several small schools were amalgamated into one large campus.

senses.definition: To mix a substance, especially a metal, with mercury to form an amalgam.
examples: The ore was crushed and then amalgamated with mercury.
Artisans once amalgamated gold to extract it from rock.

senses.definition: United or combined into one organization or structure.
examples: The amalgamated company now dominates the market.
Workers from the amalgamated union gathered in the hall.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of amalgamate: joined or mixed things into one.
examples: They amalgamated several departments last year.
The company has amalgamated with a larger rival.

senses.definition: A person or device that amalgamates, especially that mixes metals with mercury.
examples: The amalgamater oversaw the final stage of the process.
In old mining camps, an experienced amalgamater was highly valued.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of amalgamate: joins or combines things into one.
examples: The new plan amalgamates several smaller projects.
This policy amalgamates different approaches into a single framework.

senses.definition: Present participle of amalgamate: joining or combining things into one.
examples: They are amalgamating several local councils into a single authority.
The study looks at ways of amalgamating different teaching methods.

senses.definition: The process of joining two or more things, especially organizations, into one.
examples: The amalgamation of the two banks created a large new institution.
Staff were worried about job losses after the amalgamation.

senses.definition: The act of mixing a substance, especially metals, with mercury to form an amalgam.
examples: Amalgamation with mercury was once common in gold mining.
The process of amalgamation released mercury into the environment.

senses.definition: A person who supports or promotes the amalgamation of groups, organizations, or races.
examples: Amalgamationists argued for a single, unified school system.
He was criticised as an amalgamationist by his political opponents.

senses.definition: Plural of amalgamation: instances of joining organizations or things into one.
examples: Recent amalgamations have reshaped the banking sector.
Many school amalgamations took place in the 1990s.

senses.definition: Relating to or involving amalgamation; tending to combine or unite things.
examples: The amalgamative policy aimed to reduce duplication of services.
His approach is more amalgamative than confrontational.

senses.definition: To cause things to amalgamate; to combine or blend into one, used rarely and formally.
examples: The proposal sought to amalgamatize various local services.
Efforts to amalgamatize the two systems met with resistance.

senses.definition: A person, machine, or substance that causes things to amalgamate or mix together.
examples: The amalgamator combined the different metal powders.
As an effective manager, she acted as an amalgamator of diverse teams.

senses.definition: Plural of amalgamator: people or devices that cause things to amalgamate or mix together.
examples: Industrial amalgamators ensure a uniform blend of materials.
In politics, certain leaders become powerful amalgamators of opinion.

senses.definition: Someone who supports or practices amalgamation, especially social or racial mixing, often in historical contexts.
examples: Amalgamists argued that mixed communities would be stronger.
He was labelled an amalgamist by more conservative writers.

senses.definition: The act or process of amalgamating or being amalgamated, especially in technical or formal usage.
examples: The amalgamization of local services aimed to cut costs.
Critics warned that rapid amalgamization could weaken community ties.
